title: Finite-time Scaling with Arbitrary Driving Rates: Bridging the Kibble-Zurek and De Grandi-Gritsev-Polkovnikov Limits
abstract: The pursuit of a universal description for nonequilibrium critical dynamics in quantum many-body systems stands as a central frontier in modern statistical physics. For driven critical dynamics starting far from the critical point, the well-known Kibble-Zurek (KZ) scaling holds only when the driving rate lies below an upper bound. Here we study driven dynamics restricted to the critical region, and show that robust dynamic scaling behavior exists for arbitrary driving rates. We develop a generalized finite-time scaling (FTS) framework, which provides a unified understanding on the driven dynamics for the full range of quench rates, bridging the KZ scaling in the slow-driving regime and the De~Grandi-Gritsev-Polkovnikov (DGP) scaling in the sudden-quench limit. We verify this unified FTS form through numerical simulations in both quantum critical and tricritical points. The good agreement between theoretical predictions and numerical results confirms the generality of our theory. Our work establishes a universal theory for nonequilibrium critical dynamics spanning the full range of driving rates, with broad implications for quantum quench experiments and out-of-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
summary: This paper introduces a generalized Finite-Time Scaling (FTS) framework to provide a universal theory for non-equilibrium critical dynamics. It successfully unifies the Kibble-Zurek and De Grandi-Gritsev-Polkovnikov scaling limits by extending the theory to handle arbitrary driving rates within the critical region. This has broad implications for interpreting results from quantum quench experiments.
main_problem: To develop a universal description for nonequilibrium critical dynamics in quantum many-body systems that overcomes the rate limitations of existing scaling theories like Kibble-Zurek (KZ) and De Grandi-Gritsev-Polkovnikov (DGP).
main_result: The authors propose a generalized Finite-Time Scaling (FTS) framework that unifies the scaling behavior across the full range of driving rates within the critical region, valid regardless of the quench rate magnitude.
method: The theory involves developing and applying a generalized FTS form, which is then rigorously verified through numerical simulations.
model_or_system: The theory is tested on quantum many-body systems, specifically using the quantum Ising critical point and the Ising tricritical point.
key_observables: Topological defect density ($n$) and the scaling behavior of a macroscopic quantity $P$.
important_parameters: Driving rate ($R$), initial parameter ($\lambda_i$), final parameter ($\lambda_f$), and the critical point ($\lambda_c$).
main_assumptions: The generalized FTS is derived specifically for the case where the entire dynamic process, including initial and final states, remains within the critical region.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between conventional KZ dynamics (which requires rate bounds) and the generalized FTS applicable within the critical region, showing data collapse for both the quantum Ising and tricritical points.
paper_structure: The paper first identifies the limitations of existing scaling theories, then develops the generalized FTS framework, and finally verifies this unified theory through numerical simulations on specific quantum critical models.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, generalized theoretical tool for characterizing non-equilibrium dynamics in quantum systems, directly applicable to experimental quench protocols in AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Time scales in driven critical dynamics. The correlation time scale (solid curve) and the time distance to the critical point (dotted line) divide the dynamics into adiabatic and impulse regimes. (a) Conventional KZ dynamics starting far from the critical point requires the impulse regime to lie within the critical region, imposing an upper bound on the driving rate. (b) For driven dynamics initiated and evolving within the critical region, robust scaling holds for arbitrary driving rates and is described by the generalized FTS theory.

FIG. 2. Driven critical dynamics for increasing 𝜆initiated from 𝜆𝑖close to the critical point. (a) The original FTS fails to collapse data for the fixed initial parameters 𝜆𝑖= 0.999 and ℎ= 0.00005. The inset compares dynamic evolutions for different initial states at fixed ℎ= 0.00005 and 𝑅= 0.002. (b) The generalized FTS gives excellent collapse over a wide range of 𝑅for fixed 𝑔𝑖𝑅−1/𝜈𝑟≈−0.2236 and ℎ𝑅−𝛽𝛿/𝜈𝑟≈0.0167.

FIG. 3. Driven critical dynamics for decreasing 𝜆initiated from 𝜆𝑖close to the critical point. (a) The original FTS fails to collapse data for the fixed initial parameters for the fixed initial parameters 𝜆𝑖= 0.999 and ℎ= 0.00005. The inset compares dynamic evolutions for different initial states at fixed ℎ= 0.00005 and 𝑅= 0.002. (b) The generalized FTS gives excellent collapse over a wide range of 𝑅 for fixed 𝑔𝑖𝑅−1/𝜈𝑟≈−0.2236 and ℎ𝑅−𝛽𝛿/𝜈𝑟≈0.0167.

FIG. 4. Driven critical dynamics for increasing 𝜆initiated from the tricritical point. For fixed 𝑔𝑖= 0 and ℎ𝑅−𝛽𝛿/𝜈𝑟≈0.1667, rescaled curves of 𝑀versus 𝑔collapse well for a wide range of 𝑅according to the generalized FTS.
